Paragraph

Suppose that \(f\in C^{k}(D)\) and \(\bx \in D\text{.}\) Take any vector \(\bv \in \bbR^{n}\) and consider \(f(\bx+ t\bv)\) as a one variable function \(g(t)\) of \(t\) for \(t\) near \(0\text{.}\) Then by the chain rule
\begin{align*} g'(t)\amp =\sum_{j=1}^{n} v_{j}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{j}}(\bx+ t\bv)\\ g''(t)\amp =\sum_{j,k=1}^{n} v_{j}v_{i}\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{i}\partial x_{j}}(\bx+ t\bv)\\ g^{(k)}(t)\amp =\sum_{j_{1},\ldots,j_{k}=1}^{n} v_{j_{1}}\cdots v_{j_{k}}\frac{\partial^{k} f}{\partial x_{j_{k}}\cdots \partial x_{j_{1}}}(\bx+ t\bv) \end{align*}
Then the one variable Taylor expansion
\begin{equation*} g(t)=g(0)+g'(0)t+\frac{g''(0)}{2!}t^{2}+\cdots+ \frac{g^{(k)(0)}}{k!} t^{k} +R_{k+1}(t) \end{equation*}
gives rise to
\begin{align*} f(\bx+ t\bv)= \amp f(\bx)+ \sum_{j=1}^{n} t v_{j}\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{j}}(\bx)+ \frac{t^{2}}{2!} \sum_{i, j=1}^{n} v_{j}v_{i}\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{i}\partial x_{j}}(\bx)\\ \amp +\cdots + \frac{t^{k}}{k!}\sum_{j_{1},\ldots,j_{k}=1}^{n} v_{j_{1}}\cdots v_{j_{k}}\frac{\partial^{k} f}{\partial x_{j_{k}}\cdots \partial x_{j_{1}}}(\bx)+R_{k+1}(t). \end{align*}
The remainder has the property that \(\Vert R_{k+1}\Vert/\Vert \bv \Vert \to 0\) as \(\Vert \bv \Vert^{k}\to 0\text{.}\)
in-context
